title: BioSCape Coastal Carbon
authors: Guild, Liane; Maria Tzortziou
abstract: This dataset includes the spectral absorption and fluorescence characteristics of colored dissolved organic matter (CDOM) and coincident field measurements of hyperspectral water remote sensing reflectance collected in coastal and inland waters around South Africa's Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR) as part of NASA's Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) field campaign in 2023. Samples cover three ecologically distinct but socio-economically vital coastal bays: St. Helena Bay, Walker Bay, and Algoa Bay, as well as four contrasting inland aquatic systems: Rietvlei wetland, Zeekoevlei lake, Theewaterskloof dam, and the Klein River estuary. Data from this and other elements of the BioSCape project can be found via the NASA Earthdata portal (https://search.earthdata.nasa.gov).

title: The NASA Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape)
authors: Erin L. Hestir; Adam Wilson; Anabelle Cardoso; Jasper Slingsby; Cherie Forbes
abstract: NASA’s first-ever Biodiversity campaign will fly in 2023. Three imaging spectrometers and a lidar onboard two aircraft and a team of over 100 scientists will converge to advance remote sensing of terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity.

title: Increasing Data Access in Multi-Sensor Airborne Campaigns: Lessons from BioSCape in South Africa
authors: Anabelle W. Cardoso; Philip G. Brodrick; Adam M. Wilson; Jasper A. Slingsby; Cherié J. Forbes; Michele Thornton; Erin L. Hestir
abstract: BioSCape, or the Biodiversity Survey of the Cape, is NASA’s first biodiversity-focused integrated remote sensing field campaign. The campaign aims to better understand the structure, function, and composition of the region’s ecosystems, and to learn about how and why they are changing in time. To do this, airborne and field data were collected across aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems in the Greater Cape Floristic Region in southwestern South Africa in 2023. Airborne acquisitions included data from three imaging spectrometers sampling across the electromagnetic spectrum (UVSWIR and TIR) and coincident full-waveform lidar data. Field datasets included measurements to quantify the diversity of plant communities including alien invasives and kelp, phytoplankton functional types, phylogenetic histories, eDNA in watersheds, bird and frog acoustics, plant functional and spectral traits, blue carbon, and in-water radiometry. BioSCape’s airborne and field datasets are diverse and complex, and the airborne data in particular is not inherently easy to use. In this paper we outline the procedures BioSCape followed to ensure maximum impact of the data and support of Open Science and FAIR data principles.

title: Dissolved Organic Matter Dynamics in South African Nearshore Waters and Freshwater Systems: Linkages to Changing Human Activity, Episodic Events, and Biodiversity
authors: Luka Catipovic; Maria Tzortziou; Kyle J. Turner; Joshua Harringmeyer; Joaquim Goes; Helga Gomes; Jinghui Wu; Ryan E. O'Shea; Liane S. Guild; Brishan Kalyan; Lisl R. Lain; Marié E. Smith; Thomas G. Bornman; Lucienne R. D. Human; Daniel Buttner; Marelé Nel; Jeremy Kravitz; Humeshni Pilay; Samantha L. Sharp; Juan L. Torres-Perez; Glynn Pindihama; Rabelani Mudzielwana
abstract: The biodiverse and rapidly changing Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR) of southern Africa is outlined by coastal bays that receive dissolved organic matter (DOM) from rivers draining complex catchments composed of natural, agricultural, and urban land classes. As part of NASA's BioSCape field campaign (October–November 2023), we characterized the optical properties of three GCFR coastal bays (St. Helena, Walker, and Algoa) and four inland systems (Rietvlei wetland, Zeekoevlei lake, Theewaterskloof dam, and the Klein River estuary) in relation to DOM biogeochemistry and carbon cycling. Measurements of the optical properties of colored DOM (CDOM), including absorption at 300 nm (ag300), spectral slope (S275–295), and fluorescence, highlighted the bio-optical complexity associated with terrestrial influences, urban disturbances, intense biological activity, and rapid transformations along this dynamic coastline. CDOM in the coastal bays was characterized by an order of magnitude lower ag300 (0.5–2.8 m−1) and considerably higher S275–295 (0.020–0.031 nm−1) compared to upstream waters (25–71 m−1 and 0.012–0.019 nm−1, respectively), suggesting intense biological production and/or photochemical degradation. Coastal DOM was mostly (>75%) composed of protein-like compounds indicative of primary production, whereas inland DOM was mostly composed of terrigenous humic materials and had higher overall fluorescence signal. Satellite retrievals, using Sentinel-3 OLCI and Sentinel-2 MSI imagery, captured the relative influence of different rivers on coastal DOM dynamics and revealed that episodic events—extended periods of drought punctuated by heavy precipitation—are the primary drivers of biogeochemical variability along this globally significant, coastal biodiversity hotspot.

title: Practices to Decrease Parachute Science in Remote Sensing: A Case Study from South Africa
authors: Anabelle W. Cardoso; Jasper A. Slingsby; Adam M. Wilson; Philip G. Brodrick; Erin L Hestir
abstract: "Parachute science" is when research is conducted in foreign countries without engaging local people and institutions. It is a colonial practice that most often occurs when researchers from high income countries work in low and middle income countries. Remote sensing field campaigns in low and middle income countries are particularly vulnerable to parachute science. This paper reviews BioSCape, a remote sensing field campaign in South Africa, and its efforts to mitigate parachute science. We discuss strategies for equitable collaboration, trust-building, and knowledge-sharing, highlighting successes and areas for improvement that we hope can guide future campaigns towards greater inclusivity and equity.

title: BioSCape: AVIRIS-NG L3 Resampled Reflectance Mosaics, V2
authors: P.G. Brodrick; A.M. Chlus; R. Eckert; J.W. Chapman; M. Eastwood; S. Geier; M. Helmlinger; S.R. Lundeen; W. Olson-Duvall; R. Pavlick; L.M. Rios; D.R. Thompson; R.O. Green; ORNL DAAC
abstract: This dataset holds mosaics of resampled surface reflectance from the Airborne Visible / Infrared Imaging Spectrometer-Next Generation (AVIRIS-NG) instrument for Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) project. AVIRIS-NG imagery was acquired in October - November 2023 over the Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR), South Africa. L2B surface reflectance data from these AVIRIS-NG collections were resampled to 5-m spatial resolution and mosaiced into a regular tile system of 807 tiles. A given tile includes multiple AVIRIS-NG scenes from multiple flight lines spanning multiple days. BioSCape is a multi-agency, NASA-led research project that integrates airborne imaging spectroscopy and lidar with a suite of measurements of biodiversity. The dataset includes surface reflectance with uncertainty for each tile in netCDF format along with an RGB quicklook image in TIFF format. A spatial index of mosaic grid tiles is included in GeoJSON format.

title: BioSCape Estuary Wetland Vegetation Plot Data and Habitat Extents, South Africa
authors: A. Campbell; E. Adam; L. Fatoyinbo; D. J. Jensen; M. Simard; K. Smith; P. Thakali; A. Barrenblit; L. Naidoo; H. Van Deventer; A. Stovall
abstract: This dataset provides vegetation information for 64 coastal wetland plots and coastal wetland extent maps for three habitat classes in 84 estuaries in South Africa. The vegetation plot data include vegetation species occurrence, percent cover, salinity, representative vegetation heights, porewater salinity, and ground temperature. In situ plot data were collected between October 27 and November 10, 2023, in Swartvlei Estuary, Wilderness Lakes, Knynsa Estuary, and Langebaan Lagoon within lands administered by the South African National Parks. The habitat extent maps were created for 84 estuaries across the Greater Cape Floristic Region. The classification is a single point in time representing the growing season when the plot data were collected (December 2023-January 2024). The habitat extent data were created with PlanetScope satellite classified with a U-Net semantic segmentation algorithm. The habitats classified are salt marsh, submerged aquatic vegetation, and reeds & sedges. Plot and habitat data were collected and created to inform analysis of the imaging spectroscopy and LiDAR data collected as part of the BioSCape field campaign. The data are provided in cloud optimized GeoTIFF, comma separated values, and geopackage formats.

title: Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape)BioSCape Estuary Wetland Vegetation Plot Data and Habitat Extents, South Africa
authors: A. Campbell; E. Adam; L. Fatoyinbo; D.J. Jensen; M. Simard; K. Smith; P. Thakali; A. Barrenblit; L. Naidoo; H. van Deventer; A. Stovall; ORNL DAAC

title: What’s That Sound? Evaluating and Understanding the Accuracy of AI Models for Sounds and Soundscape Detection
authors: Leonard Salas; Matthew Clark; Alan Lee; Andrew Turner; Campbell Fleming; Colleen Seymour; Daniel Cloete; Darien Labbe; David Leland; Gurman Gill; John Measey; Kathy Yuen; Oliver Angus; Rose Snyder; Wendy Schackwitz
abstract: As part of the BioScape project in the Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR) of South Africa, the overall goal of the BioSoundScape project is to find effective models that accurately correlate measures of diversity among animal detections, soundscapes, and remotely-sensed vegetation spectral and structural properties. With these models, we seek to understand changes in diversity across the landscape and over time. In wet and dry season campaigns in 2023, the BioSoundScape team collected 901,987 minutes (>15,000 hours) of sound recordings from 1,206 survey locations within the flight boxes of the BioScape project. An initial component of our work is the use of Convoluted Neural Network models (CNNs) to automatically detect animal vocalizations (birds, insects, and amphibians) and broader soundscape components, namely biological sounds (biophony), anthropophony (human-caused sounds), geophony (wind, rain, etc.), and physical and electronic interference. We developed a database of human-labeled sounds on a random sample of our data, annotating the type of sound and its exact location (to the second) in >2,500 recordings. We use these reference data to test the accuracy of the CNNs. Here we report the CNN model accuracy results and optimal filters of predictions that maximize detections with minimal commission error. We also evaluate the effect of anthropophony, geophony, interference, and biophony on the accuracy of CNN-based animal predictions. We then apply the resulting optimal filters and prediction correction models to our entire dataset. We present the resulting soundscape and sound diversity estimates across all locations surveyed in the GCFR. This properly penalized and filtered set of predictions will then allow us to address project target goals of relating animal richness to habitat diversity as measured by BioSCape airborne hyperspectral and lidar sensors.

title: Thirty Years of Airborne Forest Mapping from LVIS
authors: Michelle A. Hofton; J. Bryan Blair; David Rabine
abstract: NASA's Land Vegetation and Ice Sensor (LVIS) is an airborne, wide-swath lidar system for mapping surface elevation, 3d heights and structure. Since 1996/1997 the sensor has been used to provide collect data from various biomes and locations, providing a wealth of data for a variety of applications including spaceborne sensor development and science investigations. Data are collected from medium-high altitude using footprint sizes of 5m to 25m (dependent on science requirements) and digitally-records the shapes of both the outgoing and reflecting waveforms for each laser shot. Combined with aircraft position and attitude knowledge, the sensor produces topographic maps with decimeter accuracy and vertical height and 3D structure measurements of overflown terrain, e.g., vegetation and ice. Data are freely available and distributed via the NASA NSIDC DAAC and Earthdata. LVIS has collected 100,000's sq. km over Antartica, Greenland, and the U.S. and recent campaigns include data collections in Gabon and South Africa (BioSCape) in 2023, and the US (ICESat-2 and SARP) and the Arctic (ARCSIX) in 2024. Data are used for a variety of purposes including those requiring topographic, and/or canopy/surface height measurements. LVIS is also used extensively for satellite cal/val, e.g, having flown multiple underflights of GEDI ground tracks for data product accuracy investigations. Results from recent campaigns will be highlighted as well as future campaigns and data collections opportunities discussed.

title: The Spectrum of Life: A short film about the Biodiversity Survey of the Cape
authors: Adam Wilson; Erin Lee Hestir; Jasper Slingsby; Anabelle Cardoso; Cherie Janine Forbes; Otto Whitehead; Jeremy Shelton
abstract:
All organisms have a unique fingerprint, with diverse communities on land and in water reflecting sunlight in distinctive ways. These fingerprints can provide clues about an ecosystem’s characteristics, such as how biodiverse it is, how it functions, which communities thrive in particular environments, how much chlorophyll and other pigments are present in a lake and how much water is held in a forest canopy. The link between spectral fingerprints and ecosystem characteristics unlocks the potential to measure biodiversity using NASA’s next-generation of remote sensing technology, including imaging spectroscopy.
The Spectrum of Life is a short documentary about how the BioSCape project is exploring the use of imaging spectroscopy and other tools to measure biodiversity in one of the most biodiverse regions on the planet - South Africa’s Greater Cape Floristic Region. The film features insights from and experiences with local and international researchers collaborating to push the boundaries of global biodiversity science - all weaved together with an audiovisual celebration of the region’s exceptional diversity.

title: The NASA Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape)
authors: Anabelle Cardoso; Adam Wilson; Erin Hestir; Jasper Slingsby
abstract:
We provide an overview of the first-ever NASA Biodiversity campaign, to be flown in 2023.

Quantifying ecosystem integrity is critical for biodiversity conservation and management and can be done by examining the different dimensions of biodiversity in a system. Recent and expected advances in remote sensing capabilities offer a suite of exciting opportunities to do this. Hyperspectral visible to short wavelength infrared (VSWIR) and thermal data from imaging spectrometers and laser altimeter (LiDAR) data are now being collected via satellite, and coverage and availability is only expected to increase. These data have the potential to provide information on the composition, structure, and function of ecosystems. However, the limits of hyperspectral and laser altimeter sensors to measure, monitor, and explain the drivers of taxonomic, phylogenetic, and functional diversity is yet to be established.
BioSCape, A Biodiversity Survey Of The Cape, will test the limits of what is possible in the field of remote sensing for biodiversity applications. The campaign is set to take place in South Africa's Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR), a Global Biodiversity Hotspot, and will advance our ability to measure and monitor terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity using remote sensing. During the campaign, VSWIR imaging spectroscopy data from AVIRIS-NG and PRISM will be collected alongside multispectral thermal infrared data from HyTES and LiDAR data from LVIS. Accompanying these airborne data are a variety of ground-based biodiversity-related field measurements.
Here, we present BioSCape's three major research themes: 1) the distribution and abundance of biodiversity, 2) the role of biodiversity in ecosystem function, and 3) the impacts of biodiversity change on ecosystem services. Additionally, we show how BioSCape's approach of integrating airborne data with more traditional field-based measurements will help us answer some of the most important and interesting ecological and evolutionary questions in biodiversity science.

title: Sensing Biodiversity Remotely: Updates from NASA’s First Biodiversity-Focused Airborne and Field Campaign, BioSCape I Poster
authors: Erin Lee Hestir; Adam Wilson; William Turner; Keith Gaddis; Erin Lee Hestir; Adam Wilson; William Turner; Keith Gaddis
abstract: The Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) is a concerted effort to test the limits and potential of remote sensing for biodiversity applications worldwide by combining field biodiversity measurements and local knowledge with advanced remote sensing. BioSCape is an integrated field,  airborne, and satellite remote sensing campaign that took place in South Africa in late 2023. The campaign collected airborne UVSWIR, TIR, and LiDAR data as well as a diversity of field measurements across terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems. In this session, we will hear about BioSCape’s unprecedented data set and how it brings us closer to measuring biodiversity variables globally from space.

title: Remote Sensing for Biodiversity: Insights from the BioSCape Campaign
authors: Adam Wilson; Erin Lee Hestir; Jasper Slingsby; Anabelle Cardoso
abstract: BioSCape (Biodiversity Survey of the Cape) is NASA’s first biodiversity-focused airborne and field campaign, designed to advance understanding of ecosystem structure, function, and composition, and how these are changing over time and space in South Africa’s Greater Cape Floristic Region. This international collaboration involving ~160 US and South African scientists across 19 sub-projects produced an unprecedented, open-access dataset spanning 45,000 km², integrating imaging spectroscopy (AVIRIS-NG, PRISM, HyTES), LiDAR (LVIS, ELMAP-V), and extensive ground data from ~2,600 plots. Data are publicly archived at ORNL DAAC and accessible via a cloud computing environment, fostering open science and reproducibility. In this presentation, we will summarize the results to date across the BioSCape project, highlighting key scientific outcomes from both terrestrial and aquatic landscapes. We will also discuss some of the broader implications of the project, including laying the groundwork for achieving ethical and equitable benefit-sharing via compliance with the Nagoya Protocol. A core principle of BioSCape is "use-inspired science," which involves co-developing research questions with local partners to directly address pressing conservation and resource management challenges. This ensures that our scientific findings translate into actionable data products for decision-makers in South Africa and globally. For example, BioSCape's data and products are already informing conservation and resource management efforts, including kelp range maps for ecosystem classification and woody invasive alien plant maps that feed into national ecosystem condition assessments. The project's contributions have already been recognized in the 2024 State of Environment Outlook Report for the Western Cape Province.

title: Remote Mapping of Dissolved Organic Carbon Biogeochemistry Using Imaging Spectroscopy of the Greater Cape Floristic Region, South Africa
authors: Joshua P. Harringmeyer; Luka Catipovic; Kyle Turner; Philip Brodrick; Maria Tzortziou
abstract: Coastal zones are diverse and productive regions of the land-ocean continuum, supporting rapid biogeochemical processing and material transport between terrestrial and marine ecosystems. The fate of organic carbon in these regions is poorly constrained due to spatial and temporal heterogeneity, resulting in high uncertainty in carbon fluxes along coasts. Aquatic carbon can be quantified at regional scales using ocean color remote sensing, but this approach is challenging in coastal waters containing constituents from diverse land and ocean sources with different optical properties. A new generation of hyperspectral, ocean-observing satellites in the Earth System Observatory (ESO) offer unprecedented capabilities for separating dissolved organic carbon (DOC) from other constituents in coastal waters and for characterizing the composition of dissolved organic matter. Here, we utilized airborne imaging spectroscopy from the NASA AVIRIS-NG instrument collected during the BioSCape campaign and satellite imaging spectroscopy from the NASA PACE and EMIT missions to capture DOC dynamics in the Greater Cape Floristic Region of South Africa. Using coincident measurements of in-situ spectral absorption and remote sensing reflectance collected during the BioSCape campaign, we developed algorithms that utilize high spectral resolution measurements of ocean color to retrieve DOC concentration and biogeochemical characteristics across ecologically contrasting bays and inland waters. From hyperspectral airborne imagery, we tracked organic matter from different terrestrial and marine sources in the nearshore coastal zone. Using hyperspectral satellite observations, we quantified larger-scale seasonal and disturbance-event-driven shifts in carbon concentration and biogeochemistry in South African coastal waters. Harnessing field and airborne data, particularly in under-sampled regions like southern Africa, provides opportunities to develop novel approaches to address ESO goals for global monitoring of carbon fluxes between systems, water resources, and ecosystem change.

title: Providing the BioSCape Campaign with Coordinated NASA Earthdata Dataset Curation, Publication, and Open Access through NASA DAAC’s
authors: Michele Thornton; Rupesh Shrestha; Anabelle Cardoso; Philip Brodrick
abstract:
The Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) is the first US National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) sponsored Biodiversity field campaign which took place in South Africa in late 2023 and incorporated airborne imaging spectroscopy, lidar, and a unique range of field observations and biodiversity measurements across terrestrial and aquatic environments. Airborne data were collected by NASA airborne platforms from four instruments including AVIRIS-NG, PRISM, and HyTES spectrometers and the LVIS laser altimeter. With over 150 Science Team members composed of South African and US-affiliated researchers, data collections will be substantial and heterogeneous especially considering 4 coordinated airborne instruments with flight lines across 45,000km2 of the Greater Cape Floristic Region and supporting field collections. South African partners include the South African Environmental Observation Network (SAEON) and numerous other South African conservation, research, and government institutions.
Here, we describe NASA Earthdata curation and publication of BioSCape airborne derived datasets and field data as well as supportive resources and capacity building. NASA Earthdata is the home for full and open access to NASA's Earth science data collections. As such the full suite of BioSCape airborne datasets and field data collections will be available through NASA’s archive of Earth observations. Much of the airborne and field data will be fully curated and published to NASA Earthdata through the NASA sponsored Oak Ridge National Laboratory Distributed Active Archive Center (ORNL DAAC). However, where BioSCape data are curated through other domain specific NASA DAAC’s or data-appropriate repositories, the NASA DAAC’s will coordinate through controlled Project keywords and NASA’s Common Metadata Repository (CMR) to provide users with a comprehensive listing of BioSCape collections. All datasets published through the ORNL DAAC are provided on the Earthdata Cloud which is NASA’s cloud-based archive of Earth observations and is hosted by Amazon Web Services (AWS). The ORNL DAAC will provide tutorials and training that demonstrate BioSCape airborne and field data access through programmatic discovery and access through application interfaces especially for data on NASA Earthdata Cloud.

title: Phytoplankton Community Composition in Inland Waters from Airborne and Satellite Hyperspectral Data
authors: Samantha Sharp; Ryan E. O'Shea; Liane S. Guild; Alicia Cortes; Alex Forrest; Jeremy Kravitz; Lisl Robertson-Lain; Sifiso Mpapane; Rabelani Mudzielwana; Humeshni Pillay; Glynn Pindihama; Geoffrey Schladow; Marie Smith; Juan Luis Torres-Perez
abstract: Phytoplankton Community Composition (PCC) is an important measure of the aquatic health of inland water bodies. Globally, PCC in inland waters is shifting towards Cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) dominance, resulting in toxic Harmful Algal Blooms. As such, tools for monitoring PCC are important for management of these water resources. More readily available hyperspectral data from imaging spectrometer missions, such as precursor instruments to NASA’s Surface Biology and Geology (SBG) mission, will allow for development and testing of PCC identification algorithms. This study evaluates the performance of the PCC classification algorithm Phytoplankton Detection with Optics (PHYDOTax) with new application to inland waters in California, United States and the Greater Cape Floristic Region, a biodiversity hotspot of South Africa. PHYDOTax-derived PCC is compared between in situ spectroradiometer data measured at stations in inland water sites and airborne (PRISM data from the BioSCape field campaign) and satellite hyperspectral imagery (DESIS). Additionally, in situ PCC is determined from phytoplankton pigment measurements using both CHEMTAX and phytoclass to compare with the PHYDOTax-derived PCC. We found similar results for in situ PCC derived from pigment data using CHEMTAX and phytoclass and these results were validated when compared with microscopy data available for our California inland water dataset. Our PHYDOTax results were generally successful when applied to our in situ spectral measurements especially for Cyanobacteria (R = 0.63-0.79, p<0.0001). Preliminary results show weaker performance when PHYDOTax was run on our remotely sensed hyperspectral scenes, indicating need for improved atmospheric correction and sensor sensitivity in the visible range for these optically complex aquatic targets. These initial results show promise for PHYDOTax to identify PCC in inland waters as well as the potential for airborne and satellite hyperspectral data for monitoring freshwater PCC generally.

title: Optimizing Bio-Optical Field Data Collections for Airborne Campaigns: Insights from NASA's BioSCape program in South Africa
authors: Joaquim I. Goes; Jinghui Wu; Helga R. Gomes; Thomas G. Bornman; Grant Colborne Pitcher; Charles William Kovach; Kyle C. McDonald; Maria Tzortziou; Brishan Kalyan
abstract: BioSCape is an integrated field and airborne campaign that used airborne hyperspectral data from AVIRIS-NG and PRISM aimed at mapping the biodiversity of the Cape Floristic Region of South Africa, one of the world's 36 biodiversity hotspots. It was conducted in the Fall of 2023, only a few months before the launch of NASA’s Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, ocean Ecosystem (PACE), a mission equipped with an advanced suite of hyperspectral radiometric and polarimetric instruments, aimed at revolutionizing biodiversity mapping and ecosystem change analysis. The success of PACE for developing products for mapping aquatic biodiversity and evaluating ecosystem change studies, will depend on well-designed field campaigns that maximize bio-optical data collections, essential for validating remote measurements of water-leaving radiances. Data collected during our BioSCape survey, and the lessons learned in designing and coordinating field and airborne campaigns in transitional coastal waters are of relevance to PACE and its hyperspectral capabilities especially in coastal waters. This presentation will discuss field sampling strategies employed by us to overcome the challenges of sampling in ecologically distinct bays along South Africa’s long coastline. Measurements were undertaken on two coastal survey vessels, one that allowed us to make optical and biogeochemical measurements continuously and at discrete stations along a single flight line, and the other perpendicular to the flight path, that permitted optical measurements across several flight lines. This sampling strategy planned in close coordination with the aircraft team helped maximize our bio-optical and biogeochemical data collections across diverse bio-optical water types which were vital to the campaign's success.

title: Optimization of Imaging Spectrometer Specifications for Accurate Vegetation Species Identification: A Simulation-based Approach Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Manisha Das Chaity; Jan A. van Aardt
abstract: Obtaining high-resolution spatial and spectral imagery for accurate identification of tree species in complex forest scenes remains a challenge, regardless of the progress made in remote sensing and sensor technologies. In this study, we address this gap by leveraging complex virtual environments to gain a deeper understanding of what the ideal imaging spectrometer specifications should be. We used species classification as a proxy for other imaging spectroscopy applications. The Digital Imaging and Remote Sensing Image Generation (DIRSIG) simulation environment was used to simulate data for a virtual forest scene, based on a real Harvard Forest location (MA, USA; 42°32'19.79" N, 72°10'31.81" W). This effort serves as a precursor to Mediterranean species classification, as part of NASA's BioSCape program. DIRSIG is a physics-based, first principles radiometric modeling environment for the creation of synthetic remote sensing imagery that is radiometrically, geometrically, and temporally accurate. We modeled canopy-level reflectance by integrating 3D plant models, species-level leaf reflectance, atmosphere, and sensor specifications within a synthetic forest scene. This simulation approach has facilitated comprehensive traceability of error budgets, thus ensuring high-fidelity results. We simulated image samples across a range of spatial resolutions, spectral resolutions, and sensor scale resolutions by modifying the pixel pitch and the total number of pixels in the sensor array, i.e., the focal plane dimension. We subsequently evaluated the efficacy of deep learning-based convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and traditional machine learning classifiers, such as support vector machines (SVM), for classifying forest species. Classification accuracies varied widely, ranging from 50-84%, across all resolutions, with 2-17 among 23 species classes being identified. The highest accuracy of 84% was achieved at a 3nm spectral resolution and 1m spatial resolution using CNN, while even at coarser settings of 5m spatial resolution and 20nm spectral resolution, we obtained a notable accuracy of approximately 80% with increased scale resolution. The results have been instrumental in offering invaluable insights into optimizing data collection methodologies and comprehending/understanding sensor configurations, thereby leading to an improved interpretation of spectral signatures and enhanced precision in tree species mapping within complex forest scenes. Although the primary focus of our study was species classification, this approach has potential for an extensive array of imaging spectroscopy applications, since it allows us to identify optimized sensor specifications to map and monitor ecosystems. Moreover, it can be readily adapted to explore highly diverse ecological scenarios, e.g., the BioSCape Project, focused on the fynbos biome in South Africa. This will enable us to study changing species composition over time and space, better detect invasive species, and monitor the impacts of natural disturbances, such as fire, on ecosystem diversity.

title: Optimal Temporal Windows for Mapping Fynbos Seep Wetlands Using Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Data
authors: Kevin Musungu; Moreblessings Shoko; Julian Smit
abstract: Despite growing international interest in seasonal effects on wetland vegetation mapping, there is a notable lack of research focused on South Africa’s unique fynbos wetlands, leaving a critical gap in understanding the spatiotemporal dynamics of fynbos ecosystems. This study aimed to assess the ability of Parrot Sequoia and MicaSense RedEdge-M UAV data collected during six seasonal periods between 2018 and 2020 to discriminate between fynbos wetland vegetation species. It also identifies the most suitable time of year for accurate species-level classification. The highest classification accuracy (OA = 98.0%) was achieved in late winter and early summer (OA = 90.1%), while the lowest (OA = 57.2%) occurred in mid-autumn. Most species attained high user and producer accuracies, though Erica serrata and Tetraria thermalis were more inconsistently classified. A Kruskal–Wallis test revealed a significant effect of seasonality on user and producer accuracy as well as kappa (p < 0.05). A Wilcoxon rank-sum test indicated that the accuracy metrics were not significantly different (p > 0.05) when different sensors were used within the same season. The results suggest that conservation agencies and researchers should collect remote sensing data at the end of winter to take advantage of phenological differences between plant species.

title: NASA LVIS Facility: Airborne Waveform Lidar Measurements of Surface Topography, Height and 3D Structure with Multi-sensor Emulation Capability
authors: David Rabine; Michelle A. Hofton; James Bryan Blair
abstract: NASA's Land Vegetation and Ice Sensor (LVIS) is an airborne, wide-swath lidar facility for mapping surface elevation, 3d heights and structure. Along with collocated camera images of overflown terrain, laser data are collected from medium-high altitude using footprint sizes of 5m to 25m (customized based on science requirements) and the system digitally-records the shapes of both the outgoing and reflecting waveforms for each laser shot. Combined with aircraft position and attitude knowledge, the sensor produces topographic maps with decimeter accuracy and vertical height and 3D structure measurements of overflown terrain, including sub-canopy topography, vegetation structure or ice heights. In addition to providing science-quality datasets over large areas, the Facility can be used as a technology proving ground for future space-based sensors and as a test bed for new science algorithms. Data from recent campaigns demonstrating the ability of 1064nm waveform lidar to measure surface structure will be shown, along with details of the system parameters, data collection examples, and geolocation precision and accuracy examples. The flexibility of the scanning system and beam pattern on the ground provides the ability to emulate a wide range of sensor characteristics. LVIS is the airborne emulator for the GEDI mission, providing pre-mission and on-orbit cal/val data sets, and has also collected cal/val data for ICESat and ICESat-2. Recent campaigns include data collections in Gabon (GEDI cal/val) and South Africa (BioSCape) (2023), and the US (ICESat-2 and SARP) and the Arctic (ARCSIX) (2024).

title: Monitoring Regional Biodiversity Using Scale-Normalized Metrics of Functional Richness
authors: Matthew W. Rossi; Meghan Hayden; Kyle R. Kovach; Cibele H. Amaral; Jacob Nesslage; Madeline Slimp; Rachel Meyer; Laura E. Dee; E. Natasha Stavros
abstract: Increasing access to high resolution imagery has revolutionized our ability to measure and monitor biodiversity change from above. However, important questions remain as to where, when, and at what scale functional diversity metrics derived from remote sensing data are most strongly related to in situ species diversity. To help address this challenge, we developed a new, semi-automated workflow to calculate functional metrics on large imagery datasets, and in ways that can be scaled using cloud-based computing. Specifically, we show the utility of this large-compute workflow by calculating functional richness and functional divergence at 17 sites in the National Ecological Observatory Network (NEON), at spatial scales spanning six orders of magnitude, and at sites that represent a range of land cover, climate, and geomorphological characteristics. At all sites, we show that functional divergence does not exhibit strong scale dependence. In contrast, functional richness is strongly scale dependent as observed in prior studies. The rank order of functional diversity differs among sites depending on the size of the neighborhood over which it is measured, though generally increases sub-linearly with the area. Power law regressions between area and functional richness performed better than alternatives. To help compare empirical functions within and across sites, we normalize our regressions using a representative area exponent of 0.48 (median value). This allows us to compare functional richness using a single parameter, the scaling coefficient of the normalized regression. At NEON sites, this normalized scaling coefficient is better correlated to alpha diversity in herbaceous and scrubland ecosystems than at forested sites. Regardless of ecosystem type, the normalized metric is most strongly correlated with mean annual precipitation, mean canopy height, and mean elevation. To test the utility of our new workflow, we extend this analysis to sites in the Cape Floristic Region, South Africa where NASA recently acquired comparable imagery over the Berg and Eerste watersheds as part of the BioSCape project. Specifically, we relate our new scale-normalized metric of functional richness to in situ species richness at over 30 sites where we conducted vegetation surveys and collected environmental DNA.

title: Monitoring Dimensions of Biodiversity in a Mega-Diverse Region of Southern Africa: from Traits to Communities to Ecosystems
authors: Adam M. Wilson; Jasper Slingsby; John Silander; Corey Merow; Henry Frye
abstract: The Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR) of South Africa includes marine and terrestrial biomes with species diversity rivaling mega-diverse tropical rainforests in a compact area (300x700km). Extinction risk studies suggest that GCFR species are among the most vulnerable to climate change over the next 50 years. I present a scoping proposal commissioned by NASA to develop a field campaign to measure and monitor the distribution and abundance of biodiversity with new remotely-sensed data and the rich historical data in this region. I will summarize the central questions to be addressed by this field campaign and lay out the proposed study design to integrate satellite, airborne, and in situ data collection. Our plan centers around the collection of new hyperspectral imagery from AVIRIS-NG, PRISM, and HyTES spectrometers combined with the LVIS laser altimeter. These data will be collected at approximately 20 m spatial resolution across much of the GCFR and nearby aquatic and marine ecosystems. These data will then be combined with existing and new observations of the spatial distribution of community composition and functional traits to enable high resolution mapping and modeling of several essential biodiversity variables (EBVs) including species distributions, functional traits (including leaf properties), and three-dimensional canopy structure. Given the wealth of available independent in situ data available that can be brought to bear, the GCFR is an ideal system to fully evaluate the capabilities of remote-sensing technology to characterize biodiversity patterns across diverse landscapes in a relatively compact geographic area. In combination with the rich historical data and well-developed ecological understanding in this region, these new observations will enable detailed exploration into the drivers and mechanisms of change including the feedbacks from changing biodiversity to regional climate, disturbance, post-fire recovery, freshwater provisioning, and other ecosystem services.

title: Leveraging NASA Cloud Resources to Increase Stakeholder Involvement and Inclusion in Earth Science Research
authors: Ayia Lindquist; Bryce Currey; Benjamin Poulter; Alexey N. Shiklomanov; Mark Carroll; Yoseline Angel; Evan Lang; Danl Pierce
abstract: To advance the transformation to open science (TOPS) mission, NASA's cloud-based resources such as the Science Managed Cloud Environment (SMCE) have become increasingly valuable tools. The SMCE has been utilized for multiple NASA programs, projects, and campaigns to increase accessibility, inclusion, reproducible research, and collaboration of diverse stakeholders in cutting-edge earth science research. The cloud-based environment provides capacity to overcome common barriers to research such as limited personal data storage or inefficient workflows between partners. Recent programs and campaigns that have utilized the SMCE include Surface Biology and Geology High-Frequency Time Series (SHIFT) and the Student Airborne Research Program - East (SARP-E) program, and future campaigns such as Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape), a joint campaign between NASA and the South Africa National Space Agency (SANSA), are slated to use the SMCE given its collaborative potential. Initial results from these campaigns provide promising results. While SHIFT and SARP-E engage with different stakeholders, both have benefited from the SMCE due to its highly customizable interface and multiple data storage capabilities. The seamless S3 and Distributed Active Archive Center (DAAC) integration has allowed for both SHIFT and SARP-E stakeholders to effectively utilize NASA data sets already available on the cloud without local download. The cloud environment supports SARP-E students' ability to work together simultaneously, while SHIFT leverages the cloud to store large amounts of hyperspectral data. With options for various server sizes, multiple languages kernels, and a parallel cluster available, the cloud space allows stakeholders to complete their research at varying computational levels and scales. In addition to facilitating research using large and complex datasets, these environments have also been a preview of NASA's future in the commercial cloud, giving scientists hands-on experience with cloud-based workflows and exposing NASA software engineers to scientific workflows around which to build new tooling. NASAs SMCE cloud environment has created an inclusive environment for working in cross-institutional teams and processing large amounts of data in support of NASA's TOPS mission.

title: Intrinsic Dimensionality as a metric for biodiversity: first BioSCape results
authors: Kerry Cawse-Nicholson; Nicoletta Stork; Luciana Alves; David R. Thompson; Philip Brodrick; Daniel Sousa; Moses Azong Cho; Abel Ramoelo; Philemon Tsele; Nobuhle Majozi; Cecilia Masemola; Simon Ferrier
abstract:
Current biodiversity metrics are typically applied to small local areas, require significant training data, and are not easily extensible globally. Our work will apply the mathematical concept of intrinsic dimensionality – quantifying the degrees of freedom in a process – as a method to characterize plant optical properties and quantify terrestrial biodiversity, without a training requirement. This represents the first approach that could realistically scale to map biodiversity at global scales, with metrics that are comparable across different regions and biomes. We will apply this to map alpha diversity at individual locations, as well as beta diversity across locales through Generalized Dissimilarity Models (GDMs), and gamma diversity over large regions.
We have applied this method to data acquired by the BioSCape campaign, a NASA airborne and field campaign designed to study biodiversity in the Cape Floristic Region in South Africa—one of the most biodiverse regions in the world. BioSCape included airborne acquisitions from AVIRIS-NG in late 2023. Here we present preliminary results of ID computed over the BioSCape flightlines and validated against field observations of species richness. These results show encouraging evidence that ID is a metric that captures biodiversity across different landscapes, without a training requirement.

title: Integrating Imaging Spectroscopy and Avian Functional Diversity for Biodiversity Monitoring
authors: Festus Adegbola; Adam Wilson; Stuart M. Evans; Christopher Larsen; Heather Williams
abstract: Biodiversity conservation in fire-prone landscapes demands innovative tools to monitor habitat quality and species distributions. This research investigates how plant functional traits derived from imaging spectroscopy influence avian diversity in the Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR), a globally recognized biodiversity hotspot in South Africa. Leveraging new datasets from NASA’s BioSCape mission (5 m resolution) and EMIT imaging spectrometer (60 m resolution), we explore how spectrally derived morphological and physiological traits shape bird community composition and functional guild richness across diverse habitats. We combine field-based point counts with citizen science data (eBird and SABAP2) to assess bird distributions and detect spatial patterns in functional diversity. By integrating fine- and regional-scale habitat data with fire history and avian occurrence records, this study provides a framework for mapping biodiversity and identifying conservation priorities in ecologically sensitive regions. Our work highlights the potential of remote sensing to support scalable, trait-based biodiversity monitoring and inform proactive conservation strategies in the face of environmental change.

title: Increasing data accessibility in multi-sensor airborne campaigns: Lessons from BioSCape
authors: Philip Brodrick; Erin Lee Hestir; Drew Meyers; Adam Wilson; Jasper Slingsby; Anabelle Cardoso
abstract:
The Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) campaign was an airborne and field campaign focused on understanding the structure, function, and composition of biodiversity. BioSCape’s airborne data were collected across terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems in South Africa in late 2023. Airborne data were acquired via four sensors on two aircraft: PRISM (visible to near infrared wavelengths) and AVIRIS-NG (visible to shortwave infrared wavelengths) on a Gulfstream III and HyTES (thermal infrared wavelengths) and LVIS (full waveform lidar) on a Gulfstream V. Measurements from each of these instruments are independently useful for understanding ecosystems, but together, and combined with detailed field measurements, they have immense potential to expand our understanding of what drives biodiversity patterns and processes.
Coupling data from multiple airborne data requires careful data integration - even more so when the intent is to explicitly link to field observations. Here, we present BioSCape’s efforts to harmonize data from four sensors to produce a first-of-a-kind analysis-ready dataset that can easily be integrated with field data to maximize overall scientific impact by lowering barriers to using the data. Specifically, we will present our multi-sensor common-gridded mosaiced data product from the campaign and discuss the methods used to create this and the benefits of this approach. We also discuss the open-access real-time GIS portal that underpinned flight planning and quick data access: the BioSCape Data Portal, supported by NASA’s Multi-Mission Geographic Information System (MMGIS). This level of data harmonization and commitment to Open Science is somewhat novel for an airborne campaign, and we hope to present BioSCape as a case study for improving FAIR data standards in airborne science in the future.

title: Imaging Spectroscopy Reveals Global Variation in Leaf-Economics Trait Relationships
authors: Philip A. Townsend; Ewa A. Czyz; Benjamin Dechant; Henry Frye; Kyle R. Kovach; Helene C. Muller-Landau; Prabu Ravindran; Ethan Shafron; Jasper Slingsby; Ting Zheng; Sarah Graves
abstract:
Imaging spectroscopy (IS) provides a new capacity to characterize foliar functional traits across Earth’s biomes. At present, global scale analyses of in-situ trait databases show relatively repeatable patterns of leaf economics traits, with a primary axis describing acquisitive vs conservative leaf strategies. Analyses of second and third axes yield less generalizable results attributable to both ecological and evolutionary drivers and data limitations. As well, most analyses impute missing data for infrequently measured traits and use species-level averages, thus missing within-species or among-year variation. Trait data from IS enables broad spatial coverage of a common set of traits estimated concurrently using consistent methods, that – while lacking species identities – captures within and between biome variation not feasible from field observations.

We present an analysis of IS-derived traits retrieved from acquisitions covering the Arctic to the tropics. Using PCA, both global and biome-specific analyses confirm the dominant axis to be the leaf economics spectrum from high nutrient concentrations (nitrogen and phosphorus) to high leaf mass per area (LMA). Secondary axes are more complicated to interpret, with the global dataset generally showing trade-offs between water content or sometimes pigments and leaf carbon content and related constituents. Using remote sensing to characterize leaf foliar traits, we find that the first two PCA axes generally only capture 30-60% of variation in the trait estimates, further emphasizing the potential for remote sensing to disentangle local and global tradeoffs in foliar resource allocation.

At the individual biome level, the leaf economics axes vary among biomes, with leaf defense strategies forming the secondary PCA axis in many dry seasonal biomes, while other biomes (e.g., tropics) exhibit multiple dimensions related to nutrients. Additional traits such as nonstructural carbohydrates form important axes of variation but can be correlated with or orthogonal to nutrient or defense axes, as well as being variable through time. Ultimately, foliar traits derived from imaging spectroscopy provide the opportunity to identify similarities and differences among biomes, which can serve as a basis for hypothesis testing using in-situ measurements.

title: How well can we measure plant taxonomic, phylogenetic, and functional beta-diversity using remote sensing?
authors: Xin Chen; Matt Fitzpatrick; Andrew Elmore; Daniel Spalink; John Measey; Nicola van Wilgen; Suzaan Kritzinger-Klopper; Andrew Turner; Zishan Ebrahim
abstract: The Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape) combines field sampling and airborne remote sensing in the Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR) of Southern Africa to test the potential of remote sensing to quantify biodiversity across large regions. While many studies have focused on relationships between alpha diversity and spectral diversity, little is known about how different remote sensing platforms can measure different dimensions of beta diversity - which better represents spatial variation in species composition than alpha diversity. To fill this gap, we assessed how well taxonomic, functional, and phylogenetic beta diversity can be modeled using hyperspectral and lidar data and the relative strength of these measurements in explaining different dimensions of beta diversity. While BioSCape data are being processed, we explored these questions using mature NEON datasets. We calculated taxonomic, functional, and phylogenetic beta diversity for 1,333 plant species in 1,756 plots across the eastern United States and derived 21 predictors from lidar data and 18 predictors from hyperspectral data. We fit models to each dimension of beta diversity using generalized dissimilarity modeling and assessed the relative contribution between lidar and hyperspectral predictors using variance partitioning and variable importance using permutation tests. Contrary to our expectations, deviance explained was greatest for taxonomic beta diversity (32.7%), intermediate for phylogenetic beta diversity (21.2%), and least for functional beta diversity (20.1%). Variance partitioning showed that although we found that hyperspectral consistently outperformed lidar in predicting the three dimensions of beta diversity, these different observation types contributed largely unique information, with redundancy reaching a maximum of only 10.4% for taxonomic beta diversity and a minimum of 4.2% for functional beta diversity. Our results demonstrate the ability and relative strength of hyperspectral and lidar data to predict taxonomic, phylogenetic, and functional beta diversity across large spatial scales. The modeling framework will enhance mapping different axes of beta biodiversity in GCFR featured by high levels of plant species turnover.

title: Functional Process Zone Delineation for Riverine Biodiversity Assessment: QGIS Plugin Development and Case Studies across Two Continents
authors: Jacob Nesslage; Erin Lee Hestir; Matthew W. Rossi; Rachel Meyer; E. Natasha Stavros
abstract: The concept of ‘functional process zones’ – hydrogeomorphic units of organization at valley-to-reach scales along river networks – is a useful framework for riverine biodiversity assessment. Functional process zones, defined by hydro-climatic patterns, geology, and channel and valley morphology occurring at nested, hierarchical scales, may capture how hydrogeomorphic patchiness contributes to diversity along rivers that are not well explained by continuum-related processes (e.g,. the River Continuum Concept). Yet, adoption of functional process zones for riverine applications has been limited by out of date, unsupported, or defunct spatial modeling tools (e.g., RESonate in ArcMap) and the lack of other open-source tools for derivation of the parameters needed to classify river networks into functional process zones. To this end, we developed an open-source plugin for QGIS, OpenRES, to enable automated extraction and analysis for classification of user-defined river segments into functional process zones from remote sensing datasets. The QGIS plugin enables users to automate extraction of mean annual rainfall, elevation, geology, left and right valley side slopes, down valley slope, valley width, valley floor width, and sinuosity for user-defined segments along the stream network. In addition, the plugin provides tools for unsupervised hierarchical clustering and visualization of the stream network to enable users to derive functional process zones within QGIS. The QGIS plugin and methodology developed herein is tested to assess the influence of functional process zones in juxtaposition to other spatial drivers on riverine biodiversity across two continents. Historical surveys of fish and macroinvertebrate diversity, as well as recent acquisitions of environmental DNA from soils, sediments, and water, are used to assess the spatial patterning of diversity in response to functional process zones along the Russian River watershed in California, USA, as well as the Berg and Eerste River watersheds of South Africa’s Western Cape. These efforts support research conducted by the Biodiversity across Scales team in South Africa as part of NASA’s BioSCape project, as well as California’s eDNA biodiversity surveys.

title: Facility Instruments in the Context of Field Campaigns
authors: Yaxing Wei; Michele Thornton; Scott Pearson; Bruce Wilson
abstract:
The NASA Airborne Science Program within the Earth Science Division is responsible for providing aircraft systems that further science and advance the use of satellite data. These aircraft systems, often called facility instruments, provide large remote sensing systems that support multiple science disciplines and a variety of NASA science objectives. These facility instruments and the systems that produce and provide standard levels of data products are made available to the wider NASA science community via a flight request process. Often, one or many concurrent facility instruments are employed to support the objectives of NASA-funded Earth science campaigns. The ORNL DAAC is the official archive for several facility instruments including the suite of AVIRIS (Airborne Visible/Infrared Image Spectrometer) instruments; AVIRIS-Classic, AVIRIS-Next Generation, and AVIRIS-3, as well as the MASTER (MODIS/ASTER Airborne Simulator). Other NASA DAACs are the official archive for other facility instruments.
Here we describe the use of facility instruments in the context of field campaigns and how these instruments are leveraged to address earth observation campaigns in the NASA Terrestrial Ecology and Biodiversity Programs using the NASA-funded ABoVE (Arctic-Boreal Vulnerability Experiment) and BioSCape (Biodiversity Survey of the Cape) Campaigns as examples. Each of these campaigns flew multiple, at times concurrent, facility instruments. Within each of these example campaigns, we will show how airborne facility instruments provide high resolution VSWIR and TIR image spectroscopy, radar, and laser altimeter measurements and how these data supported their campaign science objectives. We also examine how these facility instruments ultimately inform and support the development of orbital Earth Observing instrument development.

title: eDNA and Canopy Signatures Track Biodiversity Across the Western Cape's Managed and Disturbed Landscapes
authors: Madeline Slimp; Sean Johnson; Jacob Nesslage; Jabulile Malindi; Meghan Hayden; Ayesha Hargey; E. Natasha Stavros; Matthew W. Rossi; Rachel Meyer
abstract:
Traditionally, plant community research has relied on field surveys and ground measurements of environmental variables. Modern remote sensing now provides high-resolution global data, detecting plant traits and geochemical features. However, it primarily captures canopy-level information, missing smaller ecosystem components. Combining remotely sensed traits with ground-based biodiversity measurements offers opportunities for large-scale, less labor-intensive studies. Yet, we don't understand how locally measured and remotely sensed diversity metrics relate to each other or which relationships are relevant for monitoring and management.

The BioSCape program is a collaboration between U.S. and South African agencies and institutions. Using the Greater Cape Floristic Region as a study system, the project aims to understand biodiversity organization, function, and importance for ecosystem health and services. Our BioSCape project connects hyperspectral imagery with an emergent method of biodiversity quantification: environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling. The Berg and Eerste Rivers, two watershed systems sharing headwaters and surrounded by diverse land uses including agriculture, wine farms, urban areas, and pristine reserves, were surveyed using airborne data from AVIRIS-ng and PRISM imagers in 2023. 540 eDNA samples were gathered from these river systems, and DNA metabarcoding libraries were built to assess holistic biodiversity across the entire tree of life. We ask 1) Do functional and taxonomic biodiversity relate to each other along these rivers? 2) How does biodiversity organize itself along these rivers? By which dimensions? How does human intervention affect biodiversity? 3) How do invasive plant species affect non-visible biodiversity facets?

We find that taxonomic and functional diversity are related to each other across both river systems. Further, we find that biodiversity, both visible and non-visible, is organized by spectra indicative of plant health, thermal stress, and habitat structure. Invasive plants were found to have an effect on local microbial and fungal biodiversity. This holistic view of the earth has broad applications for restoration monitoring, understanding food webs and communities, and understanding the effects of natural resource management.

title: Earth Observation for Estuarine Biodiversity Monitoring: Spatial, Temporal, and Spectral Needs
authors: Anthony Campbell; Atticus Stovall; Elhadi Adam; Daniel Jensen; Marc Simard; Temilola Fatoyinbo
abstract: Estuarine biodiversity is often low due to natural stressors such as inundation and salinity. However, estuaries across South Africa have relatively high plant biodiversity and provide critical ecosystem services. Ecosystem extent mapping in these systems is often limited by the remote sensing data spatial, spectral, and temporal resolution resulting in products that exclude meaningful distinctions in habitat across the intertidal zone. The spatial and spectral resolution necessary for mapping tidal marsh ecosystems is a research gap that we seek to address. First, we compare aerial and satellite imaging spectrometer's ability to capture spectral and topographic variability across habitats at a brackish marsh in the Chesapeake Bay, MD, USA. Then we demonstrate an approach for mapping estuarine ecosystems with very high-resolution (3 m) satellite imagery and convolutional neural networks to conduct change analysis in the Greater Cape Floristic Region of South Africa and understand changes to plant functional communities in these ecosystems. We will discuss ecosystem extent change and the effect of water level on mapping in Knysna Estuary, South Africa. Tidal height significantly impacted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) values in the estuary's wetlands (R2=0.18, F(1, 306)=67.9, p<0.001). We discuss the potential of aerial imaging spectrometers, such as AVIRIS-ng, to further improve ecosystem extent mapping in the GCFR. These maps and analyses provide critical information for planning field data collection and algorithm development for imaging spectrometer data. As part of the BioSCape science team, we will endeavor to clarify the relationships between biodiversity, topography, and spectra across the estuaries of the Greater Cape Floristic Region.

title: Bringing ‘parachute science’ back to Earth to improve remote sensing of biodiversity for all
authors: Adam Wilson; Erin Hestir; Jasper Slingsby; William W. Turner; Brian Maitner; Anabelle Cardoso; Glenn Moncrieff; Abel Ramoelo; Izak Smit; Amy Frazier; Asha de Vos
abstract:
The most important scientific challenges facing us today are global in scope and interdisciplinary in nature.   Understanding and addressing environmental problems such as global climate change and biodiversity loss will require collaboration and trust within and across the international research community.   International research endeavours have the potential to engage scientists from around the world to address these questions and develop innovative solutions.  But, they also have the potential to alienate, discourage, and hinder progress through ‘parachute science’ in which scientists, often from wealthier nations, visit a location, collect data, and publish results with minimal engagement with the local scientists and/or decision-makers.  The growing use of remote sensing has the potential to exacerbate these issues through remote data collection, interpretation, and analysis.  This practice ignores local traditional knowledge and scientific expertise, reinforces existing global inequities, and reduces uptake. By ignoring critical local context and expertise, the ultimate consequences may be reduced impact of science in the local country, and lower quality science.  
Avoiding parachute science has implications not only for international science and collaborations, but also for issues of justice, equity, diversity, and inclusion (JEDI) in the US and more broadly.  Representation of racial and ethnic minorities in the geosciences remains abysmally low. Integrating local and traditional voices and perspectives through collaborative research is one way to challenge global inequities and increase diversity that can be mirrored in our student populations and future workforce. It is important to have these conversations now, as the rate of international research is increasing, particularly within the biodiversity community. A set of best practices is needed to structure projects to maximize meaningful engagement and collaboration (including opportunities for data sharing, co-authorship, reciprocal visits and training). The question is, how do we do this well?
In this workshop, we will discuss the challenges of developing bi- and multilateral international research projects, with a specific focus on avoiding ‘parachute science’ in projects related to remote sensing of the environment and biodiversity. The workshop will kick off with a panel discussion involving individuals that have had experiences with international research projects in the past. Then, using the recently funded NASA BioSCape program (bioscape.io) as an example, we will encourage participants to brainstorm and co-design strategies to enhance scientific engagement. Lastly, we will also discuss potential indicators that assess the integrity of international research projects that participants can use in their own collaborations.

title: BioSCape: Lessons on inclusive international collaboration in biodiversity research
authors: Erin Lee Hestir; Adam Wilson; Jasper Slingsby; Anabelle Cardoso; Cherie Janine Forbes; Philip Brodrick
abstract:
BioSCape is a biodiversity-focused airborne and field campaign that collected data across terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems in South Africa, with airborne acquisitions taking place in October and November 2023. The largest funder of BioSCape was NASA, a US federal institution, and the majority of research projects withinin the BioSCape Science Team are led by US-affiliated Principal Investigators. However, by design, BioSCape’s Science Team is diverse, with over 150 members, composed of approximately equal representation from South African- and US- affiliated researchers. The Science Team is also diverse in scientific discipline, proximity to end-user, field experience, local knowledge, technical capacity, and culture. However, the large proportion of US funding in BioSCape combined with the data being collected exclusively in South Africa made the campaign vulnerable to parachute science.
Being aware of this risk, BioSCape has made progress towards developing best-practices to prevent parachute science. Here, we will present our successes and lessons learned, focusing on the ways in which BioSCape promoted co-design of the research with end-users in mind and worked towards achieving Open Science, capacity building, and outreach goals. Specifically, we will discuss how co-design enhanced potential for local impact: BioSCape may contribute towards South Africa’s tracking of progress towards the goals and targets set out in the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (“The Biodiversity Plan”), to which South Africa is a signatory. We also review the ways that BioSCape ensured local expertise was incorporated in the design of both airborne and field components of the campaign, and how ethical and inclusive participation in field work was promoted and fostered across the team. We discuss how BioSCape has worked towards making all data products Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable (FAIR), especially for South African users. Finally, we share some lessons learned and ways that future international collaborations can improve upon what BioSCape achieved.

title: BioSCape: Closing the gap for global biodiversity measurements with remote sensing.
authors: Anabelle Cardoso; Adam Wilson; Erin Lee Hestir; Jasper Slingsby; Cherie Janine Forbes; Philip Brodrick
abstract:
Global biodiversity is at risk and protecting ecosystems requires accessible, reliable biodiversity data products. Creating large-scale biodiversity data products is challenging - biodiversity is complex and idiosyncratic and a one-size-fits-all approach is usually inappropriate. Recent technological advancements have expanded the suite of tools available for field biodiversity surveys, however gaps in field data persist. Next-generation remote sensing approaches, including imaging spectroscopy and LiDAR, when integrated with field data, can help fill these gaps to create scalable biodiversity data products. Despite the potential of integrated field and remote sensing datasets for global biodiversity conservation, techniques are still in development, and their limitations are poorly understood. BioSCape aims to advance remote sensing of biodiversity by integrating field, airborne, satellite, and modeled datasets and then use these to better understand where biodiversity is, how it functions, and how it might change in the future to inform conservation efforts.
Here we present the Biodiversity Survey of the Cape (BioSCape), NASA’s first biodiversity-focused airborne and field campaign taking place in South Africa. We introduce BioSCape’s Open Access airborne data sets, which include 45,000km2 of contemporaneous measurements from six instruments aboard three aircraft collected in October and November 2023. Hyperspectral measurements covering ultraviolet and visible to near-, shortwave- and thermal infrared regions were collected by NASA’s PRISM, AVIRIS-NG and HyTES instruments, while LVIS collected full-waveform LiDAR measurements. Additional discrete return LiDAR and high resolution colour-infrared photography were collected by the SAEON Airborne Remote Sensing Platform. The addition of a wide range of coincident field measurements, from vegetation and phytoplankton community data to acoustic and environmental DNA sampling, and structural information from airborne and terrestrial LiDAR data make BioSCape’s dataset unprecedented and give it immense potential to impact the field of remote sensing of biodiversity. In this presentation, we also present opportunities to engage with BioSCape and plans for the coming year.

title: BIOSCape - Mapping of phytoplankton functional types from space in support of coastal resource management and decision support activities
authors: Jinghui Wu; Joaquim I. Goes; Helga R. Gomes; Thomas G. Bornman; Grant Pitcher; Charles Kovach; Kyle Turner; Maria Tzortziou
abstract: Sheltered bays along South Africa's coastline are highly productive and its phytoplankton communities extraordinarily biodiverse. This biodiversity of organisms at the bottom of the food chain, vital for fisheries, tourism, and biodiversity, is facing increasing threats from climate change, especially extreme temperatures, hypoxia etc that are favoring outbreaks of Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs). Monitoring these detrimental changes, critical for stakeholders, remains challenging due to the ecological complexity and varying environmental conditions. As part of our BioSCAPE study, we developed a hyperspectral radiometric method that is based on the Multi-pigment inversion model (MuPI), to map the spatial distribution of phytoplankton functional types (PFTs) across environmental gradients within three ecologically distinct and socio-economically vital bays, i.e. St. Helena, Walker and Algoa. MuPI takes advantage of the unique hyperspectral remote sensing reflectance (Rrs) signals arising from phytoplankton pigment variability to detect specific PFTs. Field data, that includes optical, microscopic, phytoplankton pigment, and e-DNA data collected during the BioSCape campaign (Oct.-Nov. 2023), were used disentangle phytoplankton Rrs signatures from other seawater components like minerals and organic matter. The residual Rrs signals were then analyzed to detect different pigments, allowing discrimination of specific PFTs. MuPI was then applied to hyperspectral AVIRIS-NG and PRISM sesnors that were flown during the BIOSCape field campaign. Initial results show diatom dominance in Algoa Bay and a higher prominence of dinoflagellates in St. Helena Bay, which is consistent with the in-situ observations. Additionally, blooms of Mesodinium sp. in St. Helena Bay and Red Noctiluca scintillans in Walker Bay were captured and modeled. The model was also extended to other satellite data (Landsat OLI, Sentinel-3 OLCI, and PACE) to generate satellite maps of PFTs with associated uncertainties. These satellite maps will enhance understanding of biodiversity distribution and abundance along South Africa's coast, shedding light on the feedbacks between global change, biodiversity shifts, and ecosystem services.

title: Biodiversity Survey of the Cape ( BioSCape )
authors: Adam Wilson; Erin L. Hestir; William W. Turner; Henry Frye; Jasper Slingsby; Corey Merow; Keith Gaddis; Amy Frazier; Efthymios Ioannis Nikolopoulos
abstract: A recent report from the Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services is clear: with over a million species threatened with extinction, we are at a tipping point for biodiversity. Biodiversity loss is both a symptom and a driver of global change. Thus, detecting, understanding, and forecasting biodiversity are vital for combating contemporary loss and preventing future extinctions. This ability is especially needed within regions characterized as hotspots for biodiversity and environmental vulnerability. Mediterranean-type climate regions have already experienced significant climatic change, and climate models predict these trends will continue. In South Africa's Greater Cape Floristic Region (GCFR), the challenge is compounded by ecological feedbacks between hydrology, fire, and pressures from human populations. The GCFR has the richest temperate flora and the third-highest marine endemism in the world, containing two Global Biodiversity Hotspots, and has already experienced climate-driven changes in biodiversity. We introduce a field campaign under development with NASA that is focused on biodiversity and ecosystem forecasting in the GCFR. The campaign is organized around three major themes aimed at understanding: 1) the distribution and abundance of biodiversity, 2) the role of biodiversity in ecosystem function, and 3) the impacts of biodiversity change on ecosystem services. This focus represents an important paradigm shift from previous NASA field campaigns, which were primarily biogeochemical, toward an approach for measuring and understanding functional, phylogenetic, and taxonomic biological diversity as key components of ecosystem function. The field campaign includes collection of new hyperspectral imagery from AVIRIS-NG, PRISM, and HyTES spectrometers combined with the LVIS laser altimeter aboard the NASA GIII and GV aircraft. The field campaign is designed to improve our understanding of the drivers and mechanisms of ecological change, feedbacks between biodiversity and regional climate variability, anthropogenic and natural disturbance, post-fire recovery, freshwater provisioning, and other ecosystem services.

title: Balancing Big Data and Privacy: Sharing Biodiversity Insights from eDNA and Remote Sensing across Scales
authors: Madeline Slimp; E. Natasha Stavros; Meghan Hayden; Sean Johnson; Jacob Nesslage; Ayesha Hargey; Jabulile Malindi; Melissa Lintaar-Strauss; Anabelle Cardoso; Cherie Janine Forbes; Matthew W. Rossi; Rachel Meyer
abstract:
Integrating satellite imagery with emerging biodiversity metrics presents a challenging opportunity to advance our understanding of ecological patterns and processes. The BioSCape program aims to generate an understanding of where biodiversity is in the Western Cape of South Africa, what its function is, and why it matters for ecosystem health and services. Our BioSCape project ‘Biodiversity across Scales: Mapping Taxonomic, Phylogenetic, and Functional Diversity with eDNA, Field Surveys, and Remote Sensing Data’ forges a connection between hyperspectral imagery and another emergent method of biodiversity quantification: environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling. Our project deliverables include functional, phylogenetic, and taxonomic biodiversity maps, which inform tributary- to watershed-wide management processes. Land managers, farmers, and private business owners, while seeing value in large-scale results, are interested in data that pertains to their land. Site-specific species lists and biodiversity metrics help assess water and land health concerning the broader system but can contain sensitive data (e.g., poaching risk and/or rare species, indicators of human impact) that must be handled respecting the rights and permissions of the sample providers, and be carefully shared by project leaders.
To meet the data-sharing needs of this project, we reviewed and are incorporating lessons learned and policies from other scientific fields, like the Nagoya Protocol and the Native BioData Consortium, to meet data-sharing compliance with ethical sensitivity. We are guiding the buildout of web tools for sharing eDNA data on eDNA Explorer and, eventually, GBIF and NCBI. We are communicating early mapping results to land managers who provided samples and are doing analyses following their requests. Continual assessment of end-user wants and needs and providing education about accessing and interpreting these data types is critical for uptake. We also find it helpful to deliver results via an intuitive interactive platform for viewing, annotating, and retrieving data where managers can grant access to the data they regulate while seeing the benefits of sharing.

title: Airborne and In Situ Campaigns Cover the Earth's Climate and Floristic Variation
authors: David Schimel; Andres Baresch; Natalia Leonor Quinteros Casaverde; Yoseline Angel; Ewa A. Czyz; Erika Podest; Philip A. Townsend; K. Dana Chadwick; Kimberley Rain Miner; Shawn Serbin; Tristan Goulden; Jasper Slingsby; Ting Zheng; Henry Frye; Kyle R. Kovach; Samantha R. Weintraub-Leff; Venkata Shashank Konduri; Anabelle Cardoso; Adam M. Wilson; Kerry Cawse-Nicholson; Scott J. Goetz; Charles E. Miller; K. Fred Huemmrich; Peter C. Griffith; Philip Brodrick; Christiana Ade; John Chapman; Robert O. Green; Ryan Pavlick; William W. Turner; Michael E. Schaepman; Andreas Hueni; Michael Rast; Marco Celesti; Bimal Kumar Bhattacharya
abstract: Imaging spectroscopy from space offers a new way to study variation in the Earth's vegetation at pixel to global scales. Despite missions providing global access, retrievals are in part system-specific. The variability of vegetation phenology, leaf anatomy, chemical composition and canopy architecture means that models that relate spectral reflectance to plant characteristics requires system-specific calibration. Capturing calibration requires consistent procedures and models sensitive to local conditions. Calibration campaigns have been conducted since the early 1990s but is only recently that these projects are being conducted contemporaneously with spaceborne data, using semi-standardized methods and reporting openly available data. We analyze geographic, climatic, floristic and phylogenetic coverage of the Earth’s biomes. This session will review NASA and NSF-sponsored campaigns with talks on specific projects in a range of systems. We analyze coverage of a series of recent projects assessing progress towards calibrated and validated global retrievals of vegetation attributes. Existing in situ and remote data from recent campaigns span climate zones from tropical to arctic, many of the world’s long-separately evolved floras, and a wide range of physiognomies and functions. Metadata and new data bases allow assessing geographic and biological representativeness of existing data. Data exist for most of the branches of the angiosperm and gymnosperm tree of life, focused on overstory species.
